# **Barrington Capital Improvement Program, FY 2025**

**TO:** Roni Phipps, Brian Rua, Lawrence Bacher

**CC:** Phil Hervey, Town Manager

**FROM:** Teresa Crean, Director, Planning, Building, & Resilience

**DATE:** February 1, 2024

**RE:** Summary of Department Head Meeting with CIP Subcommittee, January 29, 2024

# **BACKGROUND**

The Capital Improvement Program (CIP) subcommittee met with various Department Heads on January 29, 2024 for presentation of each department's Capital Improvement Project Requests under the guidance of Town of Barrington, The Code, Part I, Ch. 5, Sec. 2.

Under this Chapter, the Planning Board is charged with reviewing the requests for current development plans and programs of the Town of Barrington. In evaluating such requests and making a recommendation, the Planning Board is guided by the following priorities:

- (a) Protection of life.
- (b) Maintenance of the public health.
- (c) Protection of property.
- (d) Conservation of resources.
- (e) Provision of necessary public services.
- (f) Replacement or improvements of obsolete facilities
- (g) Reduction of operating costs
- (h) Public comfort and convenience.
- (i) Provision of public recreation.
- (j) Social, cultural or aesthetic values.
- (k) Promotional values.

The review of these requests shall respect the financial resources of the town and understand the financial impact thereof. The Subcommittee was advised by the Town Manager that the total CIP Town request for FY2025 should be less than two percent (<2%) overall increase when compared the previous CIP request in FY2024. The Planning Board's Recommendation for Capital Budget and Capital Improvement Program for FY2025-FY2030 shall be presented to the Town Manager no later than February 15, 2024.

# **DEPARTMENT PRESENTATIONS**

Each department presented its fiscal year (FY) 2025 requests with explanation, analysis of remaining balance reserves and projections for FY 2026-2030; summarized as follows:

# **Police Department**

Chief Michael Correia presented the Police Department's Requests and general position seeking financing for vehicle replacement, equipment, and Public Safety Building improvements.

Chief Correia explained that with recent acquisition of electric vehicles – one Ford Mustang Mach-E and two Ford Lightning pickup trucks – this request includes the cost of two vehicles per year, the necessary equipment installations specific to outfitting police vehicles, and the required maintenance expectations (more frequent replacement of tires specific to EVs). The BPD takes two cars off the road each year, and surpluses those cars (surplus revenue goes into the general fund). There are 20 cars total in the new fleet, which is in good shape and well maintained. There are four vehicles on the Town Council agenda 2/5/24 for approval.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$125,000 for Police Auto Replacement in FY2025. (Lines GF2 in Summary Table)**

### **Police Equipment**

For the equipment requests in Table 1A, Chief Correia reviewed that armored vests have a 5-year life, computer workstation/IT upgrades are standard every year to prevent systems from becoming obsolete.

The request for Tazers is a new item. This technology has advanced, and the ongoing request will be to replace the current number of Tazers in the Police Department. The plan is to upgrade to new Tazers, phase in and then phase out the old ones. The state funded body-worn cameras, last year was the first year of the program. Upgrades are expected in 2027, and in FY2026 the Police Department will include projections for camera replacement over the next 6 year CIP, once it's determined if body cameras will be considered operating or capital expenses and costs estimates are secured to cover equipment and yearly fee to maintain/software.

Chief Correia also noted that some catastrophic items on the building budget had to come out of the equipment budget last year.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$25,500 for Police Equipment in FY2025. (Lines GF3 in Summary Table, and Table 1A)** 

## **Public Safety Building**

Chief Correia discussed the ongoing issues with HVAC systems in the Public Safety Building. Two exterior chillers were replaced last year, and the interior air handlers require replacement to address inefficiencies, ensure the system is fully operational and remediate the ongoing building condensation issues. The past chiller failure was considered catastrophic and ongoing humidity issues resulted in mold in the building.

The Town Manager noted that the building was constructed in 1999-2001, was built on a wetland, and HVAC has been an ongoing problem over the lifespan of the structure. The Town's contract energy manager, Kathy Stanley of Energy Advocates, has assembled a team to design a solution for the systems in the building. Both Chief Correia and Fire Chief Bessette are optimistic that the proposed solution by Energy Advocates will implement improvements to the system to resolve ongoing humidity issues and replace the proprietary controls. Energy Advocates is working with several departments under direction of the Town Manager to leverage grant funds and RI Energy incentives to implement a solution.

The CIP Subcommittee asked if the building has undergone a comprehensive evaluation rather than addressing the problems piecemeal. Chief Bessette noted that working recently with Energy Advocates is the first time he has felt good about the potential success of proposed building upgrades.

Additionally, the Subcommittee asked why the Police and Fire Chiefs are managing this as a facility project, and why the Town does not have a dedicated Facilities Manager to serve as the Town's point of contact for this effort. The Town Manager noted that the DPW used to have two Superintendents but one position was cut in the past, and the current DPW Superintendent wears many hats within the DPW. The CIP Subcommittee noted they could respectfully request that Town Council authorize a staffer to focus on all Facilities Management for the Town.

The CIP Subcommittee asked about an estimate for roof replacement and if a full structural assessment of the roof has been completed, noting comments from Chief Bessette that there is apparent sagging in some sections of roof. The Chiefs noted that an estimate could be secured in the coming fiscal year.

The CIP Subcommittee were in agreement that in the future, Facilities should be addressed under a separate section of the CIP for all Town assets.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$257,500 for Public Safety Building improvements requested for FY2025, including air handler/equipment, paint/gutter/window repairs, security camera replacement, roof funding, and building condensation remediation. (Lines GF5-GF9 in Summary Table)**

# **Fire Department**

Chief Gerald Bessette presented his departmental requests for Apparatus Replacement and Equipment:

In FY2023, the Capital Improvement Plan zeroed out the requested \$225K for Apparatus Replacement, but this amount was restored and approved in FY2024. After the start of FY2024, Chief Bessette learned there was an 18-month lead time for ordering this vehicle, and requested approval from the Planning Board to authorize \$250,000 in the FY2025 CIP so he could put in the order for the new vehicle with a cost of \$474,224. The Planning Board approved this request at their August 1, 2023 regular business meeting.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$250,000 for Apparatus Replacement in FY2025 to cover the cost of the new vehicle ordered for the Fire Department in August 2023. The new vehicle is expected to be delivered and operational in FY2025. (Line GF 11 in Summary Table)**

For the equipment requests in Table 1A, Chief Bessette expressed that new laptops, radios, and communications-related equipment are needed to continue implementation of system upgrades funded in FY2024. Chief Bessette underscored that the Department takes good care of equipment to ensure ongoing utility, but the Fire Department is expected to do what they need when the demand is there, which results in uncertainty with the need for equipment replacement. Additionally, standards for next round of purchases could change, bringing an increased cost.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$170,000 as requested by the Fire Department in FY2025. (Line GF12 in Summary Table)**

# **Public Works**

Director of Public Works, Alan Corvi presented his departmental requests:

### **DPW Vehicles**

All vehicles requested for FY2025 are end of life replacements, and it's important to get these orders for vehicles in as early as possible due to supply-chain issues. Three additional small dump trucks are being requested again this year, noting that three were approved for FY2024 as well. Director Corvi showed photos of the trucks that are in need of replacement, each are 12 years old.

Director Corvi also addressed that the vehicles in DPW's fleet tend to have multiple uses and applications, and they cannot predict when repairs are needed or the expected lifespan of certain vehicles due to repeated wear and tear. Backup vehicles are important when one or more are offline for repairs or routine maintenance. With this request, the number of vehicles in the DPW fleet will be nine (9): eight (8) in current fleet (includes three (3) purchased in FY2024); two (2) are end of life and will be traded in. DPW expects to replace one in 2027 and one in 2028, so all trucks in the fleet will be less than three years old.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of the full amount, \$430,000, requested by DPW for Vehicles/Small Equipment requested in FY2025. (Line GF14 in Summary Table)**

### **DPW Projects**

DPW's Bay Spring Culvert Improvement request for \$550K is requested to slip line the existing culvert, clean it out and insert a liner. Director Corvi explained that the liner hardens like a rigid plastic and allows traffic on the road during the project. In FY2024, \$50K was approved for engineering. Director Corvi noted that the contractors he spoke with provided an estimate of \$550K for engineering, and in FY2024, DPW expected this project would qualify for funding under a RIDOT Bridge funding program, so did not include the estimate for construction in the CIP. In 2023, the Town was informed by RIDOT that this project does not qualify as a bridge, so the Town is expected to cover the expense of this project.

For FY2026, DPW expects to request a new garage to house additional electric vehicles that are being secured to replace end-of-life vehicles in the fleet. DPW has one Ford Lightning EV truck, and finding housing the EVs in a heated garage will result in heightened efficiency.

**The CIP Subcommittee did not authorize any new funds for DPW projects in FY2025. The \$50,000 requested for roadway crack sealing can be funded from the balance of the reserve account, and the \$550,000 requested for the Bay Spring Culvert Improvement Design/Build can be sought through either (1) bonding, (2) grant sources, or (3) future revenues from development projects (25 Watson). (Line GF16 & GF19 in Summary Table)**

### **DPW Sewer**

Director Corvi noted that Truck 28 is their only utility truck with lights, plow etc. The existing truck does have trade in value. For future fiscal years, Director Corvi explained that the SCADA/GIS system is a computer software package that provides an alarm system to wake up staff on call for sewer-related emergencies. The current system is currently 19 years old and due for upgrade in FY 2027. The I&I study in FY2028 will update the data that feeds into requirement to file reports to US EPA for stormwater and sanitary sewers, identifying potential sources of unauthorized drainage into the system from properties in town.

The Middle Highway sewer request for FY2029 is currently a work in progress to address an undermined sewer line with an outlet elevation that is higher than the inlet elevation. Current work has involved monitoring with a pipe camera to ensure no backup with siltation/debris, but a construction budget is presented in the Sewer Fund Capital Projects chart for FY2029 to resolve the issues. There are current reserves in the sewer budget enterprise account, but additional funds may be needed to complete the project.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$100,000 for DPW Sewer Fund Capital Projects in FY2025. (Table 2)**

# **Tax Assessor**

Tax Assessor, James Drew, shared that revaluations are every 3 years, and full revaluations are every 9 years. We are in the middle of a full revaluation in FY2024, and typically \$400 - \$500K is the estimated cost for a full revaluation The existing budget will cover the current revaluation, but all additional funds will cover the 3-year revaluations, which typically cost \$350K.

The Subcommittee asked Assessor Drew if the \$140K was really needed this year, and if the annual amount being set aside this year and future years can be reduced to \$90K. Assessor Drew confirmed that \$90K will be sufficient. The approved funding was set at \$90K due to health of current reserve account balance and the work needed for 2023-2024 is currently fully funded in the Capital Reserve account. This line is setting up the funding for future revaluations.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$90,000 for Assessor-Revaluation in FY2025 and future years. (Line GF29 in Summary Table)**

# **Finance Department:**

Finance Director Kathy Raposa explained that the \$95K balance in the Town Hall Computer account will be drawn down in FY2025 to replace 38 employee workstations that are operating beyond warranty, in some cases workstations are 10 years old. Additionally, several copiers/printers are reaching end of life and require replacement.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$15,000 for Town Hall Computers in FY2025 and future years. (Line GF31 in Summary Table)**

Cybersecurity was added as an annual \$20K expense in FY2024, as the Town completed upgrades in FY2023- 2024 that included secure government-sector operating software packages for all Town employees and enhanced security for internet and wi-fi for Town offices.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$20,000 for cybersecurity in FY2025 and future years. (Line GF53 in Summary Table)** 

# **Harbormaster**

**NOTE to Planning Board Members – the Harbormaster CIP requests were not discussed with the Police Chief Correia during the CIP Subcommittee meeting, therefore will require discussion for approval at the Planning Board meeting.** 

The requests from the Police Chief include:

Harbormaster Equipment / Navigational Aids:

**Boat Replacement (Line GF33 in Summary Table) -** \$20,000 requested to support cost for a replacement vessel that is projected to be a >\$100,000 expense. The 2005 Edgewater is over 18 years old and will require funds for maintenance to support minimum level of quality function.

**Navigational Aids (Line GF34 in Summary Table) -** \$8,000 requested for 5-10 navigational aids/buoys require replacement each year, with average cost per buoy at \$1,800 each. Other funds will cover general maintenance of boat equipment and replacement engines as they age out.

### *From FY 2024:*

*Chief Correia of the Barrington Police Department presented the request for navigational aids and boat replacement. The Subcommittee asked about the type of boat needed for service on Narragansett Bay. Chief Correia stated that it's important to build up funds a future purchase. The Department has two (2) boats – one for Hundred Acre Cove and the second one for Barrington River & Narragansett Bay, which actively patrols the harbor and runs from Police Cove to the Bay. The existing boat that patrols the Bay is 18 years old and provides a lot of visibility on a busy waterway. The Committee pulled up different costs for appropriate vessels to patrol Police Cove to the Bay, and found that base price is over \$150K, so \$20K per year is warranted.*

*The Subcommittee recommended approval of \$20,000 for Motors and Boats, and \$8,000 for Navigational Aids.* 

# **Library**

Kristen Chin, Library Director, and Patrick Elliott, IT Director, presented the request for computer equipment, noting that they have access to other sources of funding and have budgeted for the same annual request over the 6-year CIP period. The Subcommittee asked if the Library is being conservative in their ask. Mr. Elliott noted that the Library can get good pricing, but supply chain issues have been a concern and computer price increases are a consideration. In the future the Library is estimating a 25% increase to keep up with changing costs.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$3,500 for FY2025, and agreed to increase the annual allocation to \$4,200 for FY2027-30. (Line GF37 in Summary Table)**

# **Recreation**

Due to an unintended scheduling error, neither the Recreation Department Director nor the Parks and Recreation Committee Chair were in attendance at the CIP Subcommittee meeting on January 29, 2024. The PBR Director contacted the Recreation Department Director after the Subcommittee Meeting to review the discussion and secure endorsement of the Director on the following.

The list of projects submitted by the Recreation Department mirrored the projects submitted for the FY2024 CIP Subcommittee review. The Town Manager was able to speak on behalf of the potential funding sources available to address the list of projects submitted for FY2025:

- Lincoln Avenue Tennis Courts, \$75,300 Town Manager noted this expenditure can be paid with existing ARPA funds.
- Shade Structure Lincoln Avenue, \$25,021 Town Manager noted this expenditure can be paid with existing Parks and Trails Capital Reserve account (Line GF-39).
- Chianese Baseball Dugout Covers, \$14,000 Town Manager noted this expenditure can be paid with Field Maintenance funds.
- For the proposed Dugout Covers and proposed Pavilion at the Barrington Middle School (BMS), the Town Manager commented there is not currently consensus among the Town and School Department for the placement of these facilities at BMS. A meeting to address these facilities is recommended to determine consensus of next steps.
- The Recreation Department also requested scoping studies for the Latham Park Concert Pavilion, and improvements at Woods Pond Park. When cost estimates are developed for these scoping studies, they can be considered for support from the Parks and Trails Capital Reserve account.

**The CIP Subcommittee recommended approval of \$65,000 for the Parks and Trails Capital Reserve Account (Line GF-39 in Summary Table) to cover costs of the Lincoln Avenue Shade Structure, Scoping Studies for Latham Park Concert Pavilion and Wood Pond Park, and to provide match dollars required for relevant recreation-related grants.**

# **Planning Department**

Teresa Crean, Town Planner, presented the Planning Department's capital requests:

**Building Official's Vehicle Replacement (Line GF38 in Summary Table) -** the CIP Subcommittee reduced this to \$0 in FY2025 since the Chevy Bolt EUV vehicle was delivered in 2023 and is in its first year of use by Town staff.

**Park and Trail improvements (Line GF39 in Summary Table)** - the CIP Subcommittee recommends \$65,000 to absorb the cost of the Lincoln Ave shade structure (see Recreation section above). The remaining funds in this account will provide local match dollars for RIDEM Recreation and Trails grants that are currently funded for Gibbs Woods (\$22,000 required match) and are proposed for RIDEM funding for the Skate Park (\$70,000 required match, if funded) and Police Cove Park splash pad improvements (\$24,200 required match, if funded).

7 **Zoning rewrite / Comp Plan Initiatives (Line GF40 in Summary Table)** - the CIP Subcommittee recommends \$40,000 for the Comprehensive Plan update, required to be completed by 2025. Director Crean secured a \$100,000 grant in 2024 from RI Housing to focus on the Housing Chapter to ensure compliance with new legislation, but \$150-175,000 will be required to hire a consultant to complete the full plan to ensure compliance with state requirements for the remaining eight topic areas covered in a Comprehensive Plan. Additionally, zoning amendments will be required upon adoption of the Comprehensive Plan, as mandated by state law that zoning ordinances are consistent with the local Comprehensive Plan.

**Energy Conservation (Line GF41 in Summary Table) -** the CIP Subcommittee recommends \$20,000 in FY2025 for ongoing support for a consulting Energy Manager, and for any projects that are identified from the Climate Action Plan, scheduled for completion December 2024.

**Land Conservation (Line GF42 in Summary Table) -** the CIP Subcommittee reduced this to \$0 in FY2025 due to a healthy capital reserve account balance to cover any surveys, appraisals, and title searches that may be required, and because there are no current plans for land acquisition by the Town.

**Town Hall Improvements (Line GF49 in Summary Table) -** the CIP Subcommittee recommends \$25,000 for the capital reserve account to support improvements to Town Hall as needed.

**Climate Mitigation Projects (Line GF52 in Summary Table) -** the CIP Subcommittee recommends \$150,000 to support ongoing projects focused on resiliency, coastal/shoreline stabilization, tree inventory implementation, Hazard Mitigation & Flood Management Plan Action Plan implementation, FEMA programs and Community Rating System efforts, and to cover increased cost of construction on existing projects and proposed projects. The existing capital reserve balance will cover the approved Climate Action Plan contract approved by Town Council January 2024, and local match requirements for FEMA, RIDEM, and RI Infrastructure Bank grant awards.

# **RECOMMENDATION**

The CIP Subcommittee recommendation for Capital Budget and Capital Improvement Program for the following Six-year period is reflected in the attached Excel Spreadsheet entitled: Capital Improvement Program – FY2025 to FY2030 with a recommended Capital Budget for FY2025 of **\$1,686,500.00.**

| 9.  | Comments                                                                                                                                           | A charging station with battery storage from an onsite solar array is proposed for<br>installation at the Bay Spring Community Center, where the vehicle can be parked to charge<br>overnight. This is proposed to illustrate a "net zero" solution for the vehicle.                                                            |     |                                                                                                                                                                                    |  |                                                                                          |                 |          |  |
